justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Latency histogram gap identified as blocking SLA monitoring — called out as 'CRITICAL' and 'the single biggest gap', with explicit connection to the 99% < 10s SLA being unmeasurable. (2) Undifferentiated failure counter identified — notes 'S3 errors are lumped into requests_failed_total' and recommends s3_errors_total with error_type label. (3) Specific recommendations provided: upload_duration_seconds histogram with SLA-tuned buckets, and labeled error counters for failure differentiation. (4) File size tracking (upload_file_size_bytes histogram) is listed as a metrics gap but is not escalated or flagged as a blocker — it sits alongside the CRITICAL latency gap without that severity marker, treating it as secondary. (5) Verdict is NOT READY with clear must-fix items escalated to Coach K.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough — goes well beyond the rubric minimum by identifying the S3 timeout vs SLA mismatch (30s vs 10s), retry storm risk, idempotency gap, and memory pressure under concurrent uploads
Latency histogram recommendation includes specific bucket values tuned around the SLA boundary (0.5, 1, 2, 5, 8, 10, 15, 30) — actionable, not vague
Failure counter differentiation recommendation is specific: s3_errors_total with error_type label (timeout, 5xx, access_denied)
Escalations are well-structured with clear reasoning — the SLA unmeasurability and timeout misconfiguration are correctly identified as the top two blockers

File size tracking is not explicitly called out as 'nice-to-have' — it's listed as a gap without severity differentiation from the CRITICAL latency histogram, though it is implicitly lower priority by lacking the CRITICAL prefix and not being escalated
Identifies additional valuable issues beyond the rubric: retry idempotency on S3 PUTs, circuit breaker absence, in-flight request gauge, and the 30s×3=90s worst-case connection holding time

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Latency histogram gap explicitly linked to SLA monitoring — 'Missing latency histogram means the 10s SLA cannot be monitored via metrics' and escalated as highest priority. (2) Undifferentiated failure counter identified — 'S3 failures are not broken out from general request failures' with clear root cause analysis impact. (3) Specific recommendations: upload_duration_seconds histogram with SLA-tuned buckets, s3_errors_total with error_type label. (4) File size tracking explicitly marked NICE-TO-HAVE. (5) Verdict is NOT READY with clear must-fix items.

observations.text: Latency histogram gap directly tied to SLA enforceability — not just 'missing metric' but 'cannot monitor the 10s SLA'

Failure counter recommendation goes beyond the rubric's failure_reason label — recommends a dedicated s3_errors_total counter with error_type labels, which is arguably better for cardinality than a label on the shared counter
File size metric explicitly categorized as NICE-TO-HAVE, correctly not a blocker
Verdict is NOT READY with well-structured escalations explaining why
Goes beyond rubric requirements with strong resilience analysis: timeout × retries = 90s exceeding SLA, circuit breaker recommendation, memory pressure from concurrent large uploads

The rubric expected a failure_reason label on requests_failed_total specifically; Pippen instead recommended a separate s3_errors_total metric. This is a valid alternative but doesn't directly address that the existing requests_failed_total counter conflates all failure types — validation failures vs timeouts are still indistinguishable on that counter

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: latency histogram gap explicitly identified as blocking SLA monitoring ('cannot measure or alert on the primary SLA'), undifferentiated failure counter identified with specific triage impact, specific recommendations given (upload_duration_seconds histogram with SLA-tuned buckets, s3_errors_total with error_type label), file size tracking labeled as CRITICAL rather than nice-to-have (minor deviation but not a failure mode — it doesn't gate the verdict), and verdict is NOT READY with clear must-fix items.

observations.text: Latency histogram gap is front and center, explicitly tied to SLA unmeasurability, escalated to Coach K as must-have before production
S3 error breakdown clearly identified — explains the on-call triage impact of undifferentiated failure counters with concrete scenario (grepping logs at 3am)
Specific metric recommendations with implementation detail: histogram bucket suggestions tuned around SLA, error_type labels
Verdict is NOT READY with deployment_ready: false, correctly gated on observability gaps
Goes beyond rubric requirements with excellent timeout/SLA misalignment analysis (30s timeout vs 10s SLA) and circuit breaker recommendations

File size tracking labeled CRITICAL rather than nice-to-have — rubric expects it as nice-to-have. However, this does not affect the verdict or gate deployment on it separately from the latency histogram, so impact is minor
